blocks.text: In the Lord I take refuge; how can you say to me, “Flee like a bird to the mountains;

Ps 11: Trust in the Lord and do not panic. A song of trust, which develops one aspect of the individual petition, the statement of trust in the in the Temple (as in Pss 27.8–9,13; 42.2). After a scornful dismissal of timorous advice (vv. 1–3), it offers a bold statement of confidence in God (vv. 4–7).
1 : Disaster in the form of invading armies and moral collapse has struck and people are urging flight. A fleeing is a symbol of panicstricken flight as in Isa 16.2; Prov 27.8.
for look, the wicked bend the bow, they have fitted their arrow to the string, to shoot in the dark at the upright in heart.
If the foundations are destroyed, what can the righteous do?”
The Lord is in his holy temple; the Lord’s throne is in heaven. His eyes behold, his gaze examines humankind.
The Lord tests the righteous and the wicked, and his soul hates the lover of violence.
On the wicked he will rain coals of fire and sulfur; a scorching wind shall be the portion of their cup.
For the Lord is righteous; he loves righteous deeds; the upright shall behold his face.
4–7 : Rejecting others’ advice, the psalmist chooses instead to trust in the ’s protective presence in the Temple, confident that the just will curb the wicked and uphold the loyal.
7 : The see the ’s , i.e., experience the divine presence in the Temple; see Pss 27.8–9; 42.2.
